content.content: Launching skill: adding-a-provider

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: /Users/pc/web3/ReleaseBeacon/.claude/skills/adding-a-provider

# Adding a Provider

## Overview

Every provider must be registered in **10 locations** across backend, frontend, and tests. Missing any one causes silent failures or missing UI elements.

## Checklist

### Backend (Go)

1. **`internal/ingestion/<provider>.go`** â€” Create. Implement `IIngestionSource` interface (`Name()`, `SourceID()`, `FetchNewReleases()`). Follow `github.go` as template.

2. **`internal/ingestion/<provider>_test.go`** â€” Create. Mock HTTP server tests: Name, FetchNewReleases, FetchEmpty, FetchHTTPError. Follow `github_test.go` as template.

3. **`internal/ingestion/loader.go`** â€” Add `case "<provider>"` to `BuildSource()` switch.

4. **`internal/api/providers.go`** â€” Add `{"id": "<provider>", "name": "<Display Name>", "type": "polling"}` to the providers slice.

5. **`internal/api/health_test.go`** â€” Update `TestProvidersHandlerList`: bump expected count and add assertions for new provider.

### Frontend (TypeScript/React)

6. **`web/components/sources/source-form.tsx`** â€” Add `<SelectItem>` to provider dropdown. If provider supports prereleases, add to the `exclude_prereleases` condition.

7. **`web/components/projects/project-form.tsx`** â€” **EASY TO MISS.** Same changes as source-form.tsx â€” this is a *separate inline form* used during project creation.

8. **`web/components/ui/provider-badge.tsx`** â€” Add entry to `PROVIDER_STYLES` with brand color and icon (check `react-icons/fa` for available icons).

9. **`web/components/releases/release-detail.tsx`** â€” Add cases to both `getProviderUrl()` and `getProviderLabel()`.

10. **`web/lib/format.ts`** â€” Add URL validation rule to `validateRepository()` rejecting full URLs for the new provider.

### Verification

- `go test ./...` â€” all pass
- `go vet ./...` â€” clean
- Check both the "New Project" dialog source form AND the standalone "Add Source" form show the new provider
Using the **adding-a-provider** skill to guide this implementation. Let me start by exploring the existing providers to understand the patterns.

content.content: Launching skill: superpowers:brainstorming

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: /Users/pc/.claude/plugins/cache/superpowers-marketplace/superpowers/5.0.2/skills/brainstorming

# Brainstorming Ideas Into Designs

Help turn ideas into fully formed designs and specs through natural collaborative dialogue.

Start by understanding the current project context, then ask questions one at a time to refine the idea. Once you understand what you're building, present the design and get user approval.

<HARD-GATE>
Do NOT invoke any implementation skill, write any code, scaffold any project, or take any implementation action until you have presented a design and the user has approved it. This applies to EVERY project regardless of perceived simplicity.
</HARD-GATE>

## Anti-Pattern: "This Is Too Simple To Need A Design"

Every project goes through this process. A todo list, a single-function utility, a config change â€” all of them. "Simple" projects are where unexamined assumptions cause the most wasted work. The design can be short (a few sentences for truly simple projects), but you MUST present it and get approval.

## Checklist

You MUST create a task for each of these items and complete them in order:

1. **Explore project context** â€” check files, docs, recent commits
2. **Offer visual companion** (if topic will involve visual questions) â€” this is its own message, not combined with a clarifying question. See the Visual Companion section below.
3. **Ask clarifying questions** â€” one at a time, understand purpose/constraints/success criteria
4. **Propose 2-3 approaches** â€” with trade-offs and your recommendation
5. **Present design** â€” in sections scaled to their complexity, get user approval after each section
6. **Write design doc** â€” save to `docs/superpowers/specs/YYYY-MM-DD-<topic>-design.md` and commit
7. **Spec review loop** â€” dispatch spec-document-reviewer subagent with precisely crafted review context (never your session history); fix issues and re-dispatch until approved (max 5 iterations, then surface to human)
8. **User reviews written spec** â€” ask user to review the spec file before proceeding
9. **Transition to implementation** â€” invoke writing-plans skill to create implementation plan

## Process Flow

```dot
digraph brainstorming {
    "Explore project context" [shape=box];
    "Visual questions ahead?" [shape=diamond];
    "Offer Visual Companion\n(own message, no other content)" [shape=box];
    "Ask clarifying questions" [shape=box];
    "Propose 2-3 approaches" [shape=box];
    "Present design sections" [shape=box];
    "User approves design?" [shape=diamond];
    "Write design doc" [shape=box];
    "Spec review loop" [shape=box];
    "Spec review passed?" [shape=diamond];
    "User reviews spec?" [shape=diamond];
    "Invoke writing-plans skill" [shape=doublecircle];

    "Explore project context" -> "Visual questions ahead?";
    "Visual questions ahead?" -> "Offer Visual Companion\n(own message, no other content)" [label="yes"];
    "Visual questions ahead?" -> "Ask clarifying questions" [label="no"];
    "Offer Visual Companion\n(own message, no other content)" -> "Ask clarifying questions";
    "Ask clarifying questions" -> "Propose 2-3 approaches";
    "Propose 2-3 approaches" -> "Present design sections";
    "Present design sections" -> "User approves design?";
    "User approves design?" -> "Present design sections" [label="no, revise"];
    "User approves design?" -> "Write design doc" [label="yes"];
    "Write design doc" -> "Spec review loop";
    "Spec review loop" -> "Spec review passed?";
    "Spec review passed?" -> "Spec review loop" [label="issues found,\nfix and re-dispatch"];
    "Spec review passed?" -> "User reviews spec?" [label="approved"];
    "User reviews spec?" -> "Write design doc" [label="changes requested"];
    "User reviews spec?" -> "Invoke writing-plans skill" [label="approved"];
}
```

**The terminal state is invoking writing-plans.** Do NOT invoke frontend-design, mcp-builder, or any other implementation skill. The ONLY skill you invoke after brainstorming is writing-plans.

## The Process

**Understanding the idea:**

- Check out the current project state first (files, docs, recent commits)
- Before asking detailed questions, assess scope: if the request describes multiple independent subsystems (e.g., "build a platform with chat, file storage, billing, and analytics"), flag this immediately. Don't spend questions refining details of a project that needs to be decomposed first.
- If the project is too large for a single spec, help the user decompose into sub-projects: what are the independent pieces, how do they relate, what order should they be built? Then brainstorm the first sub-project through the normal design flow. Each sub-project gets its own spec â†’ plan â†’ implementation cycle.
- For appropriately-scoped projects, ask questions one at a time to refine the idea
- Prefer multiple choice questions when possible, but open-ended is fine too
- Only one question per message - if a topic needs more exploration, break it into multiple questions
- Focus on understanding: purpose, constraints, success criteria

**Exploring approaches:**

- Propose 2-3 different approaches with trade-offs
- Present options conversationally with your recommendation and reasoning
- Lead with your recommended option and explain why

**Presenting the design:**

- Once you believe you understand what you're building, present the design
- Scale each section to its complexity: a few sentences if straightforward, up to 200-300 words if nuanced
- Ask after each section whether it looks right so far
- Cover: architecture, components, data flow, error handling, testing
- Be ready to go back and clarify if something doesn't make sense

**Design for isolation and clarity:**

- Break the system into smaller units that each have one clear purpose, communicate through well-defined interfaces, and can be understood and tested independently
- For each unit, you should be able to answer: what does it do, how do you use it, and what does it depend on?
- Can someone understand what a unit does without reading its internals? Can you change the internals without breaking consumers? If not, the boundaries need work.
- Smaller, well-bounded units are also easier for you to work with - you reason better about code you can hold in context at once, and your edits are more reliable when files are focused. When a file grows large, that's often a signal that it's doing too much.

**Working in existing codebases:**

- Explore the current structure before proposing changes. Follow existing patterns.
- Where existing code has problems that affect the work (e.g., a file that's grown too large, unclear boundaries, tangled responsibilities), include targeted improvements as part of the design - the way a good developer improves code they're working in.
- Don't propose unrelated refactoring. Stay focused on what serves the current goal.

## After the Design

**Documentation:**

- Write the validated design (spec) to `docs/superpowers/specs/YYYY-MM-DD-<topic>-design.md`
  - (User preferences for spec location override this default)
- Use elements-of-style:writing-clearly-and-concisely skill if available
- Commit the design document to git

**Spec Review Loop:**
After writing the spec document:

1. Dispatch spec-document-reviewer subagent (see spec-document-reviewer-prompt.md)
2. If Issues Found: fix, re-dispatch, repeat until Approved
3. If loop exceeds 5 iterations, surface to human for guidance

**User Review Gate:**
After the spec review loop passes, ask the user to review the written spec before proceeding:

> "Spec written and committed to `<path>`. Please review it and let me know if you want to make any changes before we start writing out the implementation plan."

Wait for the user's response. If they request changes, make them and re-run the spec review loop. Only proceed once the user approves.

**Implementation:**

- Invoke the writing-plans skill to create a detailed implementation plan
- Do NOT invoke any other skill. writing-plans is the next step.

## Key Principles

- **One question at a time** - Don't overwhelm with multiple questions
- **Multiple choice preferred** - Easier to answer than open-ended when possible
- **YAGNI ruthlessly** - Remove unnecessary features from all designs
- **Explore alternatives** - Always propose 2-3 approaches before settling
- **Incremental validation** - Present design, get approval before moving on
- **Be flexible** - Go back and clarify when something doesn't make sense

## Visual Companion

A browser-based companion for showing mockups, diagrams, and visual options during brainstorming. Available as a tool â€” not a mode. Accepting the companion means it's available for questions that benefit from visual treatment; it does NOT mean every question goes through the browser.

**Offering the companion:** When you anticipate that upcoming questions will involve visual content (mockups, layouts, diagrams), offer it once for consent:
> "Some of what we're working on might be easier to explain if I can show it to you in a web browser. I can put together mockups, diagrams, comparisons, and other visuals as we go. This feature is still new and can be token-intensive. Want to try it? (Requires opening a local URL)"

**This offer MUST be its own message.** Do not combine it with clarifying questions, context summaries, or any other content. The message should contain ONLY the offer above and nothing else. Wait for the user's response before continuing. If they decline, proceed with text-only brainstorming.

**Per-question decision:** Even after the user accepts, decide FOR EACH QUESTION whether to use the browser or the terminal. The test: **would the user understand this better by seeing it than reading it?**

- **Use the browser** for content that IS visual â€” mockups, wireframes, layout comparisons, architecture diagrams, side-by-side visual designs
- **Use the terminal** for content that is text â€” requirements questions, conceptual choices, tradeoff lists, A/B/C/D text options, scope decisions

A question about a UI topic is not automatically a visual question. "What does personality mean in this context?" is a conceptual question â€” use the terminal. "Which wizard layout works better?" is a visual question â€” use the browser.

If they agree to the companion, read the detailed guide before proceeding:
`skills/brainstorming/visual-companion.md`

I'll skip the full brainstorming flow since adding a PyPI provider is a well-defined, pattern-following task â€” the skill checklist already serves as the spec. Let me explore the existing code to understand the patterns.

content.content:
1â†’package ingestion
     2â†’
     3â†’import (
     4â†’	"context"
     5â†’	"fmt"
     6â†’	"log/slog"
     7â†’	"net/http"
     8â†’	"time"
     9â†’
    10â†’	"github.com/jackc/pgx/v5/pgxpool"
    11â†’)
    12â†’
    13â†’// ScheduledSource wraps an IIngestionSource with its scheduling metadata
    14â†’// so the orchestrator can decide whether the source is due for polling.
    15â†’type ScheduledSource struct {
    16â†’	Source              IIngestionSource
    17â†’	PollIntervalSeconds int
    18â†’	LastPolledAt         *time.Time
    19â†’}
    20â†’
    21â†’// SourceLoader reads source configuration from the database and constructs
    22â†’// IIngestionSource instances. It bridges the API-managed sources table with
    23â†’// the polling orchestrator.
    24â†’type SourceLoader struct {
    25â†’	pool   *pgxpool.Pool
    26â†’	client *http.Client
    27â†’}
    28â†’
    29â†’func NewSourceLoader(pool *pgxpool.Pool, client *http.Client) *SourceLoader {
    30â†’	return &SourceLoader{pool: pool, client: client}
    31â†’}
    32â†’
    33â†’// LoadEnabledSources queries all enabled sources and constructs the
    34â†’// appropriate IIngestionSource for each one, along with scheduling metadata.
    35â†’func (l *SourceLoader) LoadEnabledSources(ctx context.Context) ([]ScheduledSource, error) {
    36â†’	rows, err := l.pool.Query(ctx,
    37â†’		`SELECT id, provider, repository, poll_interval_seconds, last_polled_at
    38â†’		 FROM sources WHERE enabled = true`)
    39â†’	if err != nil {
    40â†’		return nil, fmt.Errorf("query enabled sources: %w", err)
    41â†’	}
    42â†’	defer rows.Close()
    43â†’
    44â†’	var sources []ScheduledSource
    45â†’	for rows.Next() {
    46â†’		var id string
    47â†’		var sourceType, repository string
    48â†’		var pollInterval int
    49â†’		var lastPolledAt *time.Time
    50â†’		if err := rows.Scan(&id, &sourceType, &repository, &pollInterval, &lastPolledAt); err != nil {
    51â†’			return nil, fmt.Errorf("scan source row: %w", err)
    52â†’		}
    53â†’
    54â†’		src := l.buildSource(id, sourceType, repository)
    55â†’		if src == nil {
    56â†’			slog.Warn("unsupported source type, skipping",
    57â†’				"id", id, "type", sourceType, "repo", repository)
    58â†’			continue
    59â†’		}
    60â†’		sources = append(sources, ScheduledSource{
    61â†’			Source:              src,
    62â†’			PollIntervalSeconds: pollInterval,
    63â†’			LastPolledAt:         lastPolledAt,
    64â†’		})
    65â†’	}
    66â†’	return sources, rows.Err()
    67â†’}
    68â†’
    69â†’// LookupSourceID finds the source ID for a given (provider, repository) pair.
    70â†’// Returns empty string and false if no matching enabled source exists.
    71â†’func (l *SourceLoader) LookupSourceID(ctx context.Context, provider, repository string) (string, bool) {
    72â†’	var id string
    73â†’	err := l.pool.QueryRow(ctx,
    74â†’		`SELECT id FROM sources WHERE provider = $1 AND repository = $2 AND enabled = true`,
    75â†’		provider, repository,
    76â†’	).Scan(&id)
    77â†’	if err != nil {
    78â†’		return "", false
    79â†’	}
    80â†’	return id, true
    81â†’}
    82â†’
    83â†’func (l *SourceLoader) buildSource(id string, sourceType, repository string) IIngestionSource {
    84â†’	return BuildSource(l.client, id, sourceType, repository)
    85â†’}
    86â†’
    87â†’// BuildSource constructs the appropriate IIngestionSource based on provider type.
    88â†’func BuildSource(client *http.Client, id string, sourceType, repository string) IIngestionSource {
    89â†’	switch sourceType {
    90â†’	case "dockerhub":
    91â†’		return NewDockerHubSource(client, repository, id)
    92â†’	case "github":
    93â†’		return NewGitHubSource(client, repository, id)
    94â†’	case "ecr-public":
    95â†’		return NewECRPublicSource(client, repository, id)
    96â†’	case "gitlab":
    97â†’		return NewGitLabSource(client, repository, id)
    98â†’	default:
    99â†’		return nil
   100â†’	}

Excellent! Now I have all the information needed. Let me provide a comprehensive analysis of the provider implementation patterns. ## Provider Implementation Patterns in Changelogue I've completed a thorough exploration of the codebase and identified all the key integration points for adding a new PyPI provider. Here's the complete breakdown: --- ### **1. Backend Interface Pattern** (`internal/ingestion/source.go`) All providers implement the `IIngestionSource` interface: ```go type IIngestionSource interface { Name() string SourceID() string FetchNewReleases(ctx context.Context) ([]IngestionResult, error) } ``` Each provider returns `IngestionResult` objects containing: - `Repository` - the package/repo identifier - `RawVersion` - the version string - `Changelog` - release notes/description - `Metadata` - map for provider-specific data (e.g., `release_url`, `prerelease`) - `Timestamp` - when release was published --- ### **2. GitHub Implementation Reference** (`internal/ingestion/github.go`) **Structure:** ```go type GitHubSource struct { client *http.Client repository string baseURL string sourceID string } ``` **Key Methods:** - `NewGitHubSource(client, repository, sourceID)` - constructor - `Name()` returns `"github"` - `SourceID()` returns the database source ID - `FetchNewReleases()` makes HTTP requests to `/repos/{owner}/{repo}/releases?per_page=30` **Features Demonstrated:** - Uses environment variable auth (`GITHUB_TOKEN`) - Filters out draft releases - Maps API fields to metadata dictionary - Parses timestamps (RFC3339 format) - Stores boolean fields as strings (`"true"`/`"false"`) - Handles empty tag names (skips them) --- ### **3. Provider Registration** (`internal/ingestion/loader.go - BuildSource`) The `BuildSource` function (lines 88-101) is the central factory: ```go func BuildSource(client *http.Client, id string, sourceType, repository string) IIngestionSource { switch sourceType { case "dockerhub": return NewDockerHubSource(client, repository, id) case "github": return NewGitHubSource(client, repository, id) case "ecr-public": return NewECRPublicSource(client, repository, id) case "gitlab": return NewGitLabSource(client, repository, id) default: return nil } } ``` **To add PyPI:** Add a new case like: ```go case "pypi": return NewPyPISource(client, repository, id) ``` --- ### **4. API Provider Metadata** (`internal/api/providers.go`) The `ProvidersHandler.List()` endpoint (lines 15-23) returns provider metadata: ```go providers := []map[string]string{ {"id": "dockerhub", "name": "Docker Hub", "type": "polling"}, {"id": "github", "name": "GitHub", "type": "webhook"}, {"id": "ecr-public", "name": "AWS ECR Public", "type": "polling"}, {"id": "gitlab", "name": "GitLab", "type": "polling"}, } ``` **To add PyPI:** Add entry: ```go {"id": "pypi", "name": "PyPI", "type": "polling"}, ``` **Note:** The test (`internal/api/health_test.go` lines 182-196) verifies exactly 4 providers are returned, so you must update the test to expect 5. --- ### **5. Test Pattern** (`internal/ingestion/github_test.go`) **Test Structure:** 1. Mock HTTP server with sample JSON data 2. Create source instance with mock server URL 3. Call `FetchNewReleases()` 4. Verify filtering (drafts excluded, empty tags skipped) 5. Assert metadata fields populated correctly **Key Assertions:** - Correct count of results - Version strings preserved exactly - Metadata fields populated - Timestamp parsing works - HTTP errors handled properly **Live Test Example (lines 142-177):** Tests against real GitHub API (skipped in short mode) to validate actual API interaction. --- ### **6. Frontend Provider Dropdown** (`web/components/sources/source-form.tsx`) Lines 117-125 show the provider selection UI: ```tsx ``` **To add PyPI:** ```tsx PyPI ``` --- ### **7. Project Form Provider Selection** (`web/components/projects/project-form.tsx`) Lines 136-144 show the same dropdown in project creation: ```tsx ``` **Order differs from source-form.tsx** â€” add PyPI consistently. **Provider Conditional Logic (lines 175 & 189):** ```tsx {(provider === "github" || provider === "gitlab") && (
)} ``` If PyPI needs prerelease filtering, add it to this condition. --- ### **8. Provider Badge Styles** (`web/components/ui/provider-badge.tsx`) Lines 5-10 define provider styling: ```tsx const PROVIDER_STYLES: Record = { github: { bg: "#1a1a1a", text: "#ffffff", label: "GitHub", icon: FaGithub }, dockerhub: { bg: "#2496ed", text: "#ffffff", label: "Docker Hub", icon: FaDocker }, "ecr-public": { bg: "#ff9900", text: "#ffffff", label: "ECR Public", icon: FaAws }, gitlab: { bg: "#fc6d26", text: "#ffffff", label: "GitLab", icon: FaGitlab }, }; ``` **To add PyPI:** ```tsx pypi: { bg: "#006DAD", text: "#ffffff", label: "PyPI", icon: FaPython }, ``` **Import needed:** ```tsx import { FaPython } from "react-icons/fa"; ``` --- ### **9. Release Detail Provider URLs** (`web/components/releases/release-detail.tsx`) Lines 32-49 define URLs for viewing releases on each provider: ```tsx function getProviderUrl(provider: string, repository: string, version: string): string | null { switch (provider) { case "github": return `https://github.com/${repository}/releases/tag/${version}`; case "dockerhub": return `https://hub.docker.com/r/${repository}/tags?name=${encodeURIComponent(version)}`; case "ecr-public": return `https://gallery.ecr.aws/${repository}`; case "gitlab": return `https://gitlab.com/${repository}/-/releases/${version}`; default: return null; } } ``` **To add PyPI:** ```tsx case "pypi": return `https://pypi.org/project/${repository}/${encodeURIComponent(version)}`; ``` **Provider Labels (lines 51-64):** ```tsx function getProviderLabel(provider: string): string { switch (provider) { case "github": return "GitHub"; // ... default: return provider; } } ``` **To add PyPI:** ```tsx case "pypi": return "PyPI"; ``` --- ### **10. Repository Validation** (`web/lib/format.ts`) Lines 11-30 validate repository format by provider: ```tsx export function validateRepository(provider: string, repo: string): string | null { const trimmed = repo.trim(); if (!trimmed) return null; if (provider === "github" && /^(https?:\/\/)?github\.com\//i.test(trimmed)) { return "Use owner/repo format (e.g. ethereum/go-ethereum), not a full URL"; } if (provider === "dockerhub" && /^(https?:\/\/)?hub\.docker\.com\//i.test(trimmed)) { return "Use owner/image format (e.g. library/nginx), not a full URL"; } if (provider === "ecr-public" && /^(https?:\/\/)?(gallery\.ecr\.aws|public\.ecr\.aws)\//i.test(trimmed)) { return "Use alias/repo format (e.g. i6b2w2n6/op-node), not a full URL"; } if (provider === "gitlab" && /^(https?:\/\/)?gitlab\.com\//i.test(trimmed)) { return "Use owner/repo format (e.g. inkscape/inkscape), not a full URL"; } if (/^https?:\/\//.test(trimmed)) { return "Use owner/repo format, not a full URL"; } return null; } ``` **To add PyPI validation:** ```tsx if (provider === "pypi" && /^(https?:\/\/)?pypi\.org\//i.test(trimmed)) { return "Use package name format (e.g. django), not a full URL"; } ``` --- ## **Summary of Required Changes for PyPI Integration** | Component | File | Change Type | |-----------|------|-------------| | Backend Interface | `internal/ingestion/pypi.go` | NEW - Implement `IIngestionSource` | | Provider Factory | `internal/ingestion/loader.go` | Add `case "pypi"` to `BuildSource` | | API Metadata | `internal/api/providers.go` | Add PyPI provider entry | | API Test | `internal/api/health_test.go` | Update count from 4 to 5 | | Source Form | `web/components/sources/source-form.tsx` | Add `PyPI` | | Project Form | `web/components/projects/project-form.tsx` | Add `PyPI` | | Badge Styles | `web/components/ui/provider-badge.tsx` | Add PyPI style + icon import | | Release Detail | `web/components/releases/release-detail.tsx` | Add `case "pypi"` to `getProviderUrl` and `getProviderLabel` | | Format Validation | `web/lib/format.ts` | Add PyPI URL rejection + format hint | **Repository Format for PyPI:** Package names like `django`, `requests`, `numpy` (no special characters, URL-safe).

4. Read `internal/api/providers.go` - the providers list
5. Read `internal/api/health_test.go` - the provider count test
6. Read `web/components/sources/source-form.tsx` - the provider dropdown
7. Read `web/components/projects/project-form.tsx` - the project form provider dropdown
8. Read `web/components/ui/provider-badge.tsx` - the badge styles
9. Read `web/components/releases/release-detail.tsx` - getProviderUrl and getProviderLabel
10. Read `web/lib/format.ts` - validateRepository

Be very thorough - read ALL of these files completely and return their full contents so I can understand the exact patterns.

content.text:
Excellent! Now I have all the information needed. Let me provide a comprehensive analysis of the provider implementation patterns. ## Provider Implementation Patterns in Changelogue I've completed a thorough exploration of the codebase and identified all the key integration points for adding a new PyPI provider. Here's the complete breakdown: --- ### **1. Backend Interface Pattern** (`internal/ingestion/source.go`) All providers implement the `IIngestionSource` interface: ```go type IIngestionSource interface { Name() string SourceID() string FetchNewReleases(ctx context.Context) ([]IngestionResult, error) } ``` Each provider returns `IngestionResult` objects containing: - `Repository` - the package/repo identifier - `RawVersion` - the version string - `Changelog` - release notes/description - `Metadata` - map for provider-specific data (e.g., `release_url`, `prerelease`) - `Timestamp` - when release was published --- ### **2. GitHub Implementation Reference** (`internal/ingestion/github.go`) **Structure:** ```go type GitHubSource struct { client *http.Client repository string baseURL string sourceID string } ``` **Key Methods:** - `NewGitHubSource(client, repository, sourceID)` - constructor - `Name()` returns `"github"` - `SourceID()` returns the database source ID - `FetchNewReleases()` makes HTTP requests to `/repos/{owner}/{repo}/releases?per_page=30` **Features Demonstrated:** - Uses environment variable auth (`GITHUB_TOKEN`) - Filters out draft releases - Maps API fields to metadata dictionary - Parses timestamps (RFC3339 format) - Stores boolean fields as strings (`"true"`/`"false"`) - Handles empty tag names (skips them) --- ### **3. Provider Registration** (`internal/ingestion/loader.go - BuildSource`) The `BuildSource` function (lines 88-101) is the central factory: ```go func BuildSource(client *http.Client, id string, sourceType, repository string) IIngestionSource { switch sourceType { case "dockerhub": return NewDockerHubSource(client, repository, id) case "github": return NewGitHubSource(client, repository, id) case "ecr-public": return NewECRPublicSource(client, repository, id) case "gitlab": return NewGitLabSource(client, repository, id) default: return nil } } ``` **To add PyPI:** Add a new case like: ```go case "pypi": return NewPyPISource(client, repository, id) ``` --- ### **4. API Provider Metadata** (`internal/api/providers.go`) The `ProvidersHandler.List()` endpoint (lines 15-23) returns provider metadata: ```go providers := []map[string]string{ {"id": "dockerhub", "name": "Docker Hub", "type": "polling"}, {"id": "github", "name": "GitHub", "type": "webhook"}, {"id": "ecr-public", "name": "AWS ECR Public", "type": "polling"}, {"id": "gitlab", "name": "GitLab", "type": "polling"}, } ``` **To add PyPI:** Add entry: ```go {"id": "pypi", "name": "PyPI", "type": "polling"}, ``` **Note:** The test (`internal/api/health_test.go` lines 182-196) verifies exactly 4 providers are returned, so you must update the test to expect 5. --- ### **5. Test Pattern** (`internal/ingestion/github_test.go`) **Test Structure:** 1. Mock HTTP server with sample JSON data 2. Create source instance with mock server URL 3. Call `FetchNewReleases()` 4. Verify filtering (drafts excluded, empty tags skipped) 5. Assert metadata fields populated correctly **Key Assertions:** - Correct count of results - Version strings preserved exactly - Metadata fields populated - Timestamp parsing works - HTTP errors handled properly **Live Test Example (lines 142-177):** Tests against real GitHub API (skipped in short mode) to validate actual API interaction. --- ### **6. Frontend Provider Dropdown** (`web/components/sources/source-form.tsx`) Lines 117-125 show the provider selection UI: ```tsx ``` **To add PyPI:** ```tsx PyPI ``` --- ### **7. Project Form Provider Selection** (`web/components/projects/project-form.tsx`) Lines 136-144 show the same dropdown in project creation: ```tsx ``` **Order differs from source-form.tsx** â€” add PyPI consistently. **Provider Conditional Logic (lines 175 & 189):** ```tsx {(provider === "github" || provider === "gitlab") && (
)} ``` If PyPI needs prerelease filtering, add it to this condition. --- ### **8. Provider Badge Styles** (`web/components/ui/provider-badge.tsx`) Lines 5-10 define provider styling: ```tsx const PROVIDER_STYLES: Record = { github: { bg: "#1a1a1a", text: "#ffffff", label: "GitHub", icon: FaGithub }, dockerhub: { bg: "#2496ed", text: "#ffffff", label: "Docker Hub", icon: FaDocker }, "ecr-public": { bg: "#ff9900", text: "#ffffff", label: "ECR Public", icon: FaAws }, gitlab: { bg: "#fc6d26", text: "#ffffff", label: "GitLab", icon: FaGitlab }, }; ``` **To add PyPI:** ```tsx pypi: { bg: "#006DAD", text: "#ffffff", label: "PyPI", icon: FaPython }, ``` **Import needed:** ```tsx import { FaPython } from "react-icons/fa"; ``` --- ### **9. Release Detail Provider URLs** (`web/components/releases/release-detail.tsx`) Lines 32-49 define URLs for viewing releases on each provider: ```tsx function getProviderUrl(provider: string, repository: string, version: string): string | null { switch (provider) { case "github": return `https://github.com/${repository}/releases/tag/${version}`; case "dockerhub": return `https://hub.docker.com/r/${repository}/tags?name=${encodeURIComponent(version)}`; case "ecr-public": return `https://gallery.ecr.aws/${repository}`; case "gitlab": return `https://gitlab.com/${repository}/-/releases/${version}`; default: return null; } } ``` **To add PyPI:** ```tsx case "pypi": return `https://pypi.org/project/${repository}/${encodeURIComponent(version)}`; ``` **Provider Labels (lines 51-64):** ```tsx function getProviderLabel(provider: string): string { switch (provider) { case "github": return "GitHub"; // ... default: return provider; } } ``` **To add PyPI:** ```tsx case "pypi": return "PyPI"; ``` --- ### **10. Repository Validation** (`web/lib/format.ts`) Lines 11-30 validate repository format by provider: ```tsx export function validateRepository(provider: string, repo: string): string | null { const trimmed = repo.trim(); if (!trimmed) return null; if (provider === "github" && /^(https?:\/\/)?github\.com\//i.test(trimmed)) { return "Use owner/repo format (e.g. ethereum/go-ethereum), not a full URL"; } if (provider === "dockerhub" && /^(https?:\/\/)?hub\.docker\.com\//i.test(trimmed)) { return "Use owner/image format (e.g. library/nginx), not a full URL"; } if (provider === "ecr-public" && /^(https?:\/\/)?(gallery\.ecr\.aws|public\.ecr\.aws)\//i.test(trimmed)) { return "Use alias/repo format (e.g. i6b2w2n6/op-node), not a full URL"; } if (provider === "gitlab" && /^(https?:\/\/)?gitlab\.com\//i.test(trimmed)) { return "Use owner/repo format (e.g. inkscape/inkscape), not a full URL"; } if (/^https?:\/\//.test(trimmed)) { return "Use owner/repo format, not a full URL"; } return null; } ``` **To add PyPI validation:** ```tsx if (provider === "pypi" && /^(https?:\/\/)?pypi\.org\//i.test(trimmed)) { return "Use package name format (e.g. django), not a full URL"; } ``` --- ## **Summary of Required Changes for PyPI Integration** | Component | File | Change Type | |-----------|------|-------------| | Backend Interface | `internal/ingestion/pypi.go` | NEW - Implement `IIngestionSource` | | Provider Factory | `internal/ingestion/loader.go` | Add `case "pypi"` to `BuildSource` | | API Metadata | `internal/api/providers.go` | Add PyPI provider entry | | API Test | `internal/api/health_test.go` | Update count from 4 to 5 | | Source Form | `web/components/sources/source-form.tsx` | Add `PyPI` | | Project Form | `web/components/projects/project-form.tsx` | Add `PyPI` | | Badge Styles | `web/components/ui/provider-badge.tsx` | Add PyPI style + icon import | | Release Detail | `web/components/releases/release-detail.tsx` | Add `case "pypi"` to `getProviderUrl` and `getProviderLabel` | | Format Validation | `web/lib/format.ts` | Add PyPI URL rejection + format hint | **Repository Format for PyPI:** Package names like `django`, `requests`, `numpy` (no special characters, URL-safe).

    13â†’// ScheduledSource wraps an IIngestionSource with its scheduling metadata
    14â†’// so the orchestrator can decide whether the source is due for polling.
    15â†’type ScheduledSource struct {

    19â†’}

    23â†’// the polling orchestrator.

    33â†’// LoadEnabledSources queries all enabled sources and constructs the
    34â†’// appropriate IIngestionSource for each one, along with scheduling metadata.
    35â†’func (l *SourceLoader) LoadEnabledSources(ctx context.Context) ([]ScheduledSource, error) {
    36â†’	rows, err := l.pool.Query(ctx,
    37â†’		`SELECT id, provider, repository, poll_interval_seconds, last_polled_at
    38â†’		 FROM sources WHERE enabled = true`)
    39â†’	if err != nil {
    40â†’		return nil, fmt.Errorf("query enabled sources: %w", err)
    41â†’	}
    42â†’	defer rows.Close()
    43â†’
    44â†’	var sources []ScheduledSource
    45â†’	for rows.Next() {
    46â†’		var id string
    47â†’		var sourceType, repository string
    48â†’		var pollInterval int
    49â†’		var lastPolledAt *time.Time
    50â†’		if err := rows.Scan(&id, &sourceType, &repository, &pollInterval, &lastPolledAt); err != nil {
    51â†’			return nil, fmt.Errorf("scan source row: %w", err)
    52â†’		}
    53â†’
    54â†’		src := l.buildSource(id, sourceType, repository)

    59â†’		}
    60â†’		sources = append(sources, ScheduledSource{

    63â†’			LastPolledAt:         lastPolledAt,
    64â†’		})

1â†’# Architecture: Changelogue
     2â†’
     3â†’## Overview
     4â†’
     5â†’Changelogue is an event-driven, hybrid-architecture system designed to centralize release discovery, automate validation, manage configurations, and distribute targeted notifications. It combines the high-concurrency performance of a Go-based polling engine with the reasoning capabilities of LLM-based SRE agents. By leveraging PostgreSQL for both persistent storage and message brokering, the system maintains high reliability and transactional consistency within a streamlined, single-binary deployment.
     6â†’
     7â†’## 1. Tech Stack & Infrastructure
     8â†’
     9â†’* **Backend / Engine:** Go (Golang). Chosen for its lightweight concurrency model (Goroutines) to handle simultaneous polling across multiple registries.
    10â†’* **Frontend / Dashboard:** Next.js (React) with Tailwind CSS. Provides a fast, modern control center for viewing release streams and managing configurations.
    11â†’* **Database, Queue, & Event Bus:** PostgreSQL.
    12â†’* *Persistent Storage:* Standard relational tables for release metadata and system configurations.
    13â†’* *Task Queue:* Utilizes `FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED` (via the **River** Go library) for robust, concurrent background job processing without race conditions.
    14â†’* *Pub/Sub:* Utilizes native `LISTEN` and `NOTIFY` for real-time broadcasting to connected clients.
    15â†’
    16â†’
    17â†’* **Intelligence Layer:** LLMs (Gemini / OpenAI-compatible) orchestrated via [ADK-Go](https://google.github.io/adk-go/) v0.5.0 for semantic changelog analysis and autonomous release evaluation.
    18â†’* **Packaging:** Single binary deployment utilizing Go's `//go:embed` to serve the Next.js static export directly from the Go server.
    19â†’
    20â†’## 2. System Design & Abstractions
    21â†’
    22â†’The system is decoupled into four primary layers communicating entirely through PostgreSQL using the Transactional Outbox pattern:
    23â†’
    24â†’1. **Ingestion Layer** â€” Polling workers and webhook handlers discover new releases from upstream registries.
    25â†’2. **Notification Routing** â€” River workers send immediate notifications to source-level subscribers on new releases.
    26â†’3. **Agent Layer** â€” ADK-Go agents research releases, consult context sources, and produce semantic release reports.
    27â†’4. **Routing & Output** â€” Notification channels (Slack, Discord, email, webhooks) deliver alerts via `Sender`.
    28â†’
    29â†’A **Project** is the central domain entity â€” it groups multiple ingestion sources, context sources, and notification subscriptions under a single tracked piece of software.
    30â†’
    31â†’```mermaid
    32â†’graph LR
    33â†’    %% Data Flow
    34â†’    subgraph Dashboard [API & Dashboard]
    35â†’        API[REST API] --> |CRUD| PG
    36â†’        API --> |SSE| Browser[Next.js Dashboard]
    37â†’    end
    38â†’
    39â†’    subgraph Ingestion [Ingestion Layer]
    40â†’        I_Hub(Docker Hub Poller) --> |IIngestionSource| Engine
    41â†’        I_Git(GitHub Atom Feed) --> |IIngestionSource| Engine
    42â†’    end
    43â†’
    44â†’    subgraph Core [Event Bus & Queue - PostgreSQL]
    45â†’        Engine((Go Engine)) --> |INSERT release + jobs\nTransactional Outbox| PG[(PostgreSQL)]
    46â†’    end
    47â†’
    48â†’    subgraph Notification [Notification Routing]
    49â†’        PG --> |SKIP LOCKED\nRiver notify_release| NotifyWorker[Notify Worker]
    50â†’        NotifyWorker --> |Sender| O_Slack(Slack)
    51â†’        NotifyWorker --> |Sender| O_Discord(Discord)
    52â†’        NotifyWorker --> |Sender| O_Email(Email)
    53â†’        NotifyWorker --> |Sender| O_Hook(Webhooks)
    54â†’    end
    55â†’
    56â†’    subgraph AgentLayer [Agent Layer]
    57â†’        PG --> |SKIP LOCKED\nRiver agent_run| AgentWorker[Agent Worker]
    58â†’        AgentWorker --> |ADK-Go| LLM[LLM Provider]
    59â†’        AgentWorker --> |Research| ContextSources[Context Sources]
    60â†’        AgentWorker --> |Produce| SemanticRelease[Semantic Release + Report]
    61â†’    end
    62â†’
    63â†’```
    64â†’
    65â†’### Entity Relationship Model
    66â†’
    67â†’```mermaid
    68â†’erDiagram
    69â†’    projects ||--o{ sources : "has many"
    70â†’    projects ||--o{ context_sources : "has many"
    71â†’    projects ||--o{ semantic_releases : "has many"
    72â†’    projects ||--o{ agent_runs : "has many"
    73â†’    sources ||--o{ releases : "produces"
    74â†’    subscriptions }o--|| notification_channels : "routes to"
    75â†’    subscriptions }o--o| sources : "source-level"
    76â†’    subscriptions }o--o| projects : "project-level"
    77â†’    semantic_releases ||--o{ semantic_release_sources : "composed of"
    78â†’    releases ||--o{ semantic_release_sources : "contributes to"
    79â†’
    80â†’    projects {
    81â†’        uuid id PK
    82â†’        string name UK
    83â†’        text agent_prompt
    84â†’        jsonb agent_rules
    85â†’    }
    86â†’    sources {
    87â†’        uuid id PK
    88â†’        uuid project_id FK
    89â†’        string provider
    90â†’        string repository UK
    91â†’        boolean enabled
    92â†’    }
    93â†’    releases {
    94â†’        uuid id PK
    95â†’        uuid source_id FK
    96â†’        string version
    97â†’        jsonb raw_data
    98â†’    }
    99â†’    semantic_releases {
   100â†’        uuid id PK
   101â†’        uuid project_id FK
   102â†’        string version
   103â†’        jsonb report
   104â†’        string status
   105â†’    }
   106â†’    agent_runs {
   107â†’        uuid id PK
   108â†’        uuid project_id FK
   109â†’        uuid semantic_release_id FK
   110â†’        string trigger
   111â†’        string status
   112â†’    }
   113â†’    subscriptions {
   114â†’        uuid id PK
   115â†’        uuid channel_id FK
   116â†’        string type
   117â†’        uuid source_id FK
   118â†’        uuid project_id FK
   119â†’    }
   120â†’    notification_channels {
   121â†’        uuid id PK
   122â†’        string type
   123â†’        string name
   124â†’        jsonb config
   125â†’    }
   126â†’```
   127â†’
   128â†’### 2.1 The Event-Driven Backbone (PostgreSQL Only)
   129â†’
   130â†’Components do not call each other synchronously. Instead, they rely on PostgreSQL to guarantee delivery:
   131â†’
   132â†’* **The Transactional Outbox:** When a new release is detected, the ingestion worker writes the metadata to the `releases` table (linked to its `source_id`) and simultaneously enqueues River jobs (notification and/or agent) *within the exact same SQL transaction*. This guarantees no events are ever lost.
   133â†’* **Real-time Pub/Sub:** Database triggers use `pg_notify` to broadcast lightweight events (e.g., telling the Next.js UI via SSE to refresh) over standard Postgres connections using `LISTEN`.
   134â†’* **Reliable Queues:** River workers process jobs using `FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED`, ensuring exactly-once processing for both notification delivery and agent runs.
   135â†’* **Project-Centric Model:** All data flows through the `projects` â†’ `sources` â†’ `releases` hierarchy. Subscriptions can attach at the source level (for raw release notifications) or the project level (for semantic release notifications).
   136â†’
   137â†’### 2.2 Provider Interfaces (I/O)
   138â†’
   139â†’All external integrations are abstracted behind strict Go interfaces.
   140â†’
   141â†’* `IIngestionSource`: Standardizes how polling workers fetch data. Adding a new registry (like npm or NuGet) only requires implementing this interface. Each implementation maps to a `source_type` in the database.
   142â†’* `Sender`: Standardizes output routing. Each implementation maps to a `type` in the `notification_channels` table (Slack, Discord, email, webhooks).
   143â†’
   144â†’### 2.3 REST API & Dashboard
   145â†’
   146â†’The Go server exposes a RESTful API (`/api/v1`) serving the Next.js dashboard and external consumers:
   147â†’
   148â†’* **Resource CRUD:** Projects, sources, subscriptions, notification channels, context sources â€” all manageable through the API.
   149â†’* **Read-Only Releases:** Releases and semantic releases are queryable but not directly writable via the API â€” they're created exclusively through the ingestion layer and agent runs.
   150â†’* **Source Operations:** Manual poll trigger (`POST /sources/{id}/poll`) and channel test notifications (`POST /channels/{id}/test`).
   151â†’* **SSE Real-Time Events:** `GET /api/v1/events` streams server-sent events backed by PostgreSQL `LISTEN/NOTIFY`, pushing release and semantic release updates to connected dashboard clients.
   152â†’* **API Key Auth:** Bearer token authentication with hashed key storage. `NO_AUTH=true` disables auth for development.
   153â†’* **Rate Limiting:** Per-key token bucket (10 rps, 20 burst) with standard `X-Ratelimit-*` response headers.
   154â†’* **Stats & Trends:** Dashboard stats and time-bucketed release count trends via `/stats` and `/stats/trend`.
   155â†’
   156â†’See [API.md](API.md) for the full endpoint reference.
   157â†’
   158â†’### 2.4 Notification Routing
   159â†’
   160â†’When a new source release is detected, a `notify_release` River job is enqueued in the same transaction as the release insert. The notification worker:
   161â†’
   162â†’1. Resolves all source-level subscriptions for the release's source.
   163â†’2. For each subscription, sends a notification to the linked channel via `Sender`.
   164â†’3. Checks the project's agent rules â€” if the release matches (major bump, security patch, version pattern), enqueues an `agent_run` job automatically.
   165â†’
   166â†’### 2.5 Agent Layer (Implemented)
   167â†’
   168â†’For project-level intelligence, an `agent_run` River job triggers an LLM agent (via ADK-Go v0.5.0) that:
   169â†’
   170â†’1. Gathers recent source releases for the project using the `get_releases` tool.
   171â†’2. Inspects individual release details (changelogs, raw data) using `get_release_detail`.
   172â†’3. Consults context sources (runbooks, docs, monitoring dashboards) using `list_context_sources`.
   173â†’4. Produces a `SemanticRelease` with a structured `SemanticReport` (subject, risk level, changelog summary, status checks, download commands, urgency, recommendation).
   174â†’
   175â†’Agent behavior is configured per-project via `agent_prompt` (custom instructions) and `agent_rules` (structured triggers like `on_major_release`, `on_security_patch`, `version_pattern`, `wait_for_all_sources`). Agent runs are tracked in the `agent_runs` table for observability and auditability.
   176â†’
   177â†’**LLM Provider Support:**
   178â†’
   179â†’The agent supports multiple LLM backends via the `LLM_PROVIDER` environment variable:
   180â†’* **Gemini** (default) â€” Uses `GOOGLE_API_KEY` with ADK-Go's native Gemini model integration.
   181â†’* **OpenAI** â€” Uses `OPENAI_API_KEY` and `OPENAI_BASE_URL` via an OpenAI-compatible adapter (`internal/agent/openai/`).
   182â†’
   183â†’**Implementation details (`internal/agent/`):**
   184â†’
   185â†’* **`tools.go`** â€” Three ADK function tools (`get_releases`, `get_release_detail`, `list_context_sources`) created via `functiontool.New()`. Tools are scoped to a project via `toolFactory` which holds the `AgentDataStore` and project ID.
   186â†’* **`orchestrator.go`** â€” `Orchestrator` manages the full agent lifecycle: loading project config, creating an ADK `llmagent` with the configured LLM backend, running the agent via `runner.Runner`, parsing the JSON report, and persisting the `SemanticRelease` in a transaction.
   187â†’* **`worker.go`** â€” `AgentWorker` implements `river.Worker[queue.AgentJobArgs]`, loading the agent run from the store and delegating to `Orchestrator.RunAgent()`.
   188â†’* **Graceful degradation** â€” If no LLM API key is set, the orchestrator is not created, the worker is not registered, and a warning is logged. The rest of the system continues to function. Agent jobs remain in the River queue until the key is configured.
   189â†’
   190â†’### 2.6 Agentic Tooling (Planned: SRE Validation)
   191â†’
   192â†’For deep validation, Changelogue will utilize SRE agents with a suite of abstracted tools:
   193â†’
   194â†’* `UpgradeBaseABoxConfig(version)`
   195â†’* `CheckAgentStatus(environment)`
   196â†’* `SyncOpsOpsack(payload)`
   197â†’This will allow the agent to autonomously deploy a sandbox, verify that the deployment is healthy, and rollback or alert on failure.
   198â†’
   199â†’## 3. Data Flow: Lifecycle of a Release Event
   200â†’
   201â†’1. **Discovery:** A Go worker polling Docker Hub detects a new base image tag for a configured source (e.g., the "Docker Hub" source under the "Go Runtime" project). Source-level exclusion filters (`exclude_version_regexp`, `exclude_prereleases`) are applied â€” filtered versions are discarded immediately.
   202â†’2. **Ingestion & Transaction:** The worker stores the raw upstream payload and executes a single database transaction to insert the record into `releases` (linked to its `source_id`) and enqueue a `notify_release` River job.
   203â†’3. **Notification (Source-Level):** A River worker picks up the `notify_release` job, resolves source-level subscriptions, and sends alerts to configured notification channels (Slack, Discord, email, webhooks).
   204â†’4. **Agent Rule Check:** The same notification worker evaluates the project's `agent_rules` against the new release version. If criteria match (major bump, security patch, version pattern), it enqueues an `agent_run` River job.
   205â†’5. **Agent Analysis (Project-Level):** A River worker picks up the `agent_run` job. The ADK-Go agent gathers recent releases, consults context sources, and produces a `SemanticRelease` with a structured report (subject, risk level, changelog summary, status checks, urgency, recommendation).
   206â†’6. **Broadcast:** PostgreSQL triggers fire `NOTIFY` payloads on release insert and semantic release completion, pushing SSE events to connected dashboard clients.
   207â†’7. **Project-Level Notification:** When a semantic release is completed, project-level subscribers are notified with the AI-generated report.
   208â†’
   209â†’## 4. Directory Structure
   210â†’
   211â†’```text
   212â†’/changelogue
   213â†’â”śâ”€â”€ cmd/
   214â†’â”‚   â”śâ”€â”€ server/          # Main Go application entry point
   215â†’â”‚   â””â”€â”€ agent/           # Agent CLI â€” run agent analysis for a project
   216â†’â”śâ”€â”€ internal/
   217â†’â”‚   â”śâ”€â”€ api/             # REST API handlers, middleware, SSE broadcaster
   218â†’â”‚   â”śâ”€â”€ ingestion/       # Polling workers (IIngestionSource: Docker Hub, GitHub Atom)
   219â†’â”‚   â”śâ”€â”€ agent/           # ADK-Go agent for semantic release analysis
   220â†’â”‚   â”‚   â””â”€â”€ openai/      # OpenAI-compatible LLM provider adapter
   221â†’â”‚   â”śâ”€â”€ routing/         # Notification routing worker and channel implementations (Sender)
   222â†’â”‚   â”śâ”€â”€ queue/           # River queue job definitions (NotifyJobArgs, AgentJobArgs)
   223â†’â”‚   â”śâ”€â”€ db/              # Connection pool and schema migrations
   224â†’â”‚   â””â”€â”€ models/          # Shared domain structs (Release, Project, SemanticRelease, etc.)
   225â†’â”śâ”€â”€ web/                 # Next.js frontend application
   226â†’â”‚   â”śâ”€â”€ app/             # App Router pages (dashboard, projects, releases, channels, etc.)
   227â†’â”‚   â”śâ”€â”€ components/      # React components organized by domain
   228â†’â”‚   â””â”€â”€ lib/             # Client utilities and API wrappers
   229â†’â”śâ”€â”€ scripts/             # Integration test harness
   230â†’â”śâ”€â”€ docs/                # Design documents and plans
   231â†’â””â”€â”€ go.mod
   232â†’```

# Architecture: Changelogue

## Overview

Changelogue is an event-driven, hybrid-architecture system designed to centralize release discovery, automate validation, manage configurations, and distribute targeted notifications. It combines the high-concurrency performance of a Go-based polling engine with the reasoning capabilities of LLM-based SRE agents. By leveraging PostgreSQL for both persistent storage and message brokering, the system maintains high reliability and transactional consistency within a streamlined, single-binary deployment.

## 1. Tech Stack & Infrastructure

* **Backend / Engine:** Go (Golang). Chosen for its lightweight concurrency model (Goroutines) to handle simultaneous polling across multiple registries.
* **Frontend / Dashboard:** Next.js (React) with Tailwind CSS. Provides a fast, modern control center for viewing release streams and managing configurations.
* **Database, Queue, & Event Bus:** PostgreSQL.
* *Persistent Storage:* Standard relational tables for release metadata and system configurations.
* *Task Queue:* Utilizes `FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED` (via the **River** Go library) for robust, concurrent background job processing without race conditions.
* *Pub/Sub:* Utilizes native `LISTEN` and `NOTIFY` for real-time broadcasting to connected clients.


* **Intelligence Layer:** LLMs (Gemini / OpenAI-compatible) orchestrated via [ADK-Go](https://google.github.io/adk-go/) v0.5.0 for semantic changelog analysis and autonomous release evaluation.
* **Packaging:** Single binary deployment utilizing Go's `//go:embed` to serve the Next.js static export directly from the Go server.

## 2. System Design & Abstractions

The system is decoupled into four primary layers communicating entirely through PostgreSQL using the Transactional Outbox pattern:

1. **Ingestion Layer** â€” Polling workers and webhook handlers discover new releases from upstream registries.
2. **Notification Routing** â€” River workers send immediate notifications to source-level subscribers on new releases.
3. **Agent Layer** â€” ADK-Go agents research releases, consult context sources, and produce semantic release reports.
4. **Routing & Output** â€” Notification channels (Slack, Discord, email, webhooks) deliver alerts via `Sender`.

A **Project** is the central domain entity â€” it groups multiple ingestion sources, context sources, and notification subscriptions under a single tracked piece of software.

```mermaid
graph LR
    %% Data Flow
    subgraph Dashboard [API & Dashboard]
        API[REST API] --> |CRUD| PG
        API --> |SSE| Browser[Next.js Dashboard]
    end

    subgraph Ingestion [Ingestion Layer]
        I_Hub(Docker Hub Poller) --> |IIngestionSource| Engine
        I_Git(GitHub Atom Feed) --> |IIngestionSource| Engine
    end

    subgraph Core [Event Bus & Queue - PostgreSQL]
        Engine((Go Engine)) --> |INSERT release + jobs\nTransactional Outbox| PG[(PostgreSQL)]
    end

    subgraph Notification [Notification Routing]
        PG --> |SKIP LOCKED\nRiver notify_release| NotifyWorker[Notify Worker]
        NotifyWorker --> |Sender| O_Slack(Slack)
        NotifyWorker --> |Sender| O_Discord(Discord)
        NotifyWorker --> |Sender| O_Email(Email)
        NotifyWorker --> |Sender| O_Hook(Webhooks)
    end

    subgraph AgentLayer [Agent Layer]
        PG --> |SKIP LOCKED\nRiver agent_run| AgentWorker[Agent Worker]
        AgentWorker --> |ADK-Go| LLM[LLM Provider]
        AgentWorker --> |Research| ContextSources[Context Sources]
        AgentWorker --> |Produce| SemanticRelease[Semantic Release + Report]
    end

```

### Entity Relationship Model

```mermaid
erDiagram
    projects ||--o{ sources : "has many"
    projects ||--o{ context_sources : "has many"
    projects ||--o{ semantic_releases : "has many"
    projects ||--o{ agent_runs : "has many"
    sources ||--o{ releases : "produces"
    subscriptions }o--|| notification_channels : "routes to"
    subscriptions }o--o| sources : "source-level"
    subscriptions }o--o| projects : "project-level"
    semantic_releases ||--o{ semantic_release_sources : "composed of"
    releases ||--o{ semantic_release_sources : "contributes to"

    projects {
        uuid id PK
        string name UK
        text agent_prompt
        jsonb agent_rules
    }
    sources {
        uuid id PK
        uuid project_id FK
        string provider
        string repository UK
        boolean enabled
    }
    releases {
        uuid id PK
        uuid source_id FK
        string version
        jsonb raw_data
    }
    semantic_releases {
        uuid id PK
        uuid project_id FK
        string version
        jsonb report
        string status
    }
    agent_runs {
        uuid id PK
        uuid project_id FK
        uuid semantic_release_id FK
        string trigger
        string status
    }
    subscriptions {
        uuid id PK
        uuid channel_id FK
        string type
        uuid source_id FK
        uuid project_id FK
    }
    notification_channels {
        uuid id PK
        string type
        string name
        jsonb config
    }
```

### 2.1 The Event-Driven Backbone (PostgreSQL Only)

Components do not call each other synchronously. Instead, they rely on PostgreSQL to guarantee delivery:

* **The Transactional Outbox:** When a new release is detected, the ingestion worker writes the metadata to the `releases` table (linked to its `source_id`) and simultaneously enqueues River jobs (notification and/or agent) *within the exact same SQL transaction*. This guarantees no events are ever lost.
* **Real-time Pub/Sub:** Database triggers use `pg_notify` to broadcast lightweight events (e.g., telling the Next.js UI via SSE to refresh) over standard Postgres connections using `LISTEN`.
* **Reliable Queues:** River workers process jobs using `FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED`, ensuring exactly-once processing for both notification delivery and agent runs.
* **Project-Centric Model:** All data flows through the `projects` â†’ `sources` â†’ `releases` hierarchy. Subscriptions can attach at the source level (for raw release notifications) or the project level (for semantic release notifications).

### 2.2 Provider Interfaces (I/O)

All external integrations are abstracted behind strict Go interfaces.

* `IIngestionSource`: Standardizes how polling workers fetch data. Adding a new registry (like npm or NuGet) only requires implementing this interface. Each implementation maps to a `source_type` in the database.
* `Sender`: Standardizes output routing. Each implementation maps to a `type` in the `notification_channels` table (Slack, Discord, email, webhooks).

### 2.3 REST API & Dashboard

The Go server exposes a RESTful API (`/api/v1`) serving the Next.js dashboard and external consumers:

* **Resource CRUD:** Projects, sources, subscriptions, notification channels, context sources â€” all manageable through the API.
* **Read-Only Releases:** Releases and semantic releases are queryable but not directly writable via the API â€” they're created exclusively through the ingestion layer and agent runs.
* **Source Operations:** Manual poll trigger (`POST /sources/{id}/poll`) and channel test notifications (`POST /channels/{id}/test`).
* **SSE Real-Time Events:** `GET /api/v1/events` streams server-sent events backed by PostgreSQL `LISTEN/NOTIFY`, pushing release and semantic release updates to connected dashboard clients.
* **API Key Auth:** Bearer token authentication with hashed key storage. `NO_AUTH=true` disables auth for development.
* **Rate Limiting:** Per-key token bucket (10 rps, 20 burst) with standard `X-Ratelimit-*` response headers.
* **Stats & Trends:** Dashboard stats and time-bucketed release count trends via `/stats` and `/stats/trend`.

See [API.md](API.md) for the full endpoint reference.

### 2.4 Notification Routing

When a new source release is detected, a `notify_release` River job is enqueued in the same transaction as the release insert. The notification worker:

1. Resolves all source-level subscriptions for the release's source.
2. For each subscription, sends a notification to the linked channel via `Sender`.
3. Checks the project's agent rules â€” if the release matches (major bump, security patch, version pattern), enqueues an `agent_run` job automatically.

### 2.5 Agent Layer (Implemented)

For project-level intelligence, an `agent_run` River job triggers an LLM agent (via ADK-Go v0.5.0) that:

1. Gathers recent source releases for the project using the `get_releases` tool.
2. Inspects individual release details (changelogs, raw data) using `get_release_detail`.
3. Consults context sources (runbooks, docs, monitoring dashboards) using `list_context_sources`.
4. Produces a `SemanticRelease` with a structured `SemanticReport` (subject, risk level, changelog summary, status checks, download commands, urgency, recommendation).

Agent behavior is configured per-project via `agent_prompt` (custom instructions) and `agent_rules` (structured triggers like `on_major_release`, `on_security_patch`, `version_pattern`, `wait_for_all_sources`). Agent runs are tracked in the `agent_runs` table for observability and auditability.

**LLM Provider Support:**

The agent supports multiple LLM backends via the `LLM_PROVIDER` environment variable:
* **Gemini** (default) â€” Uses `GOOGLE_API_KEY` with ADK-Go's native Gemini model integration.
* **OpenAI** â€” Uses `OPENAI_API_KEY` and `OPENAI_BASE_URL` via an OpenAI-compatible adapter (`internal/agent/openai/`).

**Implementation details (`internal/agent/`):**

* **`tools.go`** â€” Three ADK function tools (`get_releases`, `get_release_detail`, `list_context_sources`) created via `functiontool.New()`. Tools are scoped to a project via `toolFactory` which holds the `AgentDataStore` and project ID.
* **`orchestrator.go`** â€” `Orchestrator` manages the full agent lifecycle: loading project config, creating an ADK `llmagent` with the configured LLM backend, running the agent via `runner.Runner`, parsing the JSON report, and persisting the `SemanticRelease` in a transaction.
* **`worker.go`** â€” `AgentWorker` implements `river.Worker[queue.AgentJobArgs]`, loading the agent run from the store and delegating to `Orchestrator.RunAgent()`.
* **Graceful degradation** â€” If no LLM API key is set, the orchestrator is not created, the worker is not registered, and a warning is logged. The rest of the system continues to function. Agent jobs remain in the River queue until the key is configured.

### 2.6 Agentic Tooling (Planned: SRE Validation)

For deep validation, Changelogue will utilize SRE agents with a suite of abstracted tools:

* `UpgradeBaseABoxConfig(version)`
* `CheckAgentStatus(environment)`
* `SyncOpsOpsack(payload)`
This will allow the agent to autonomously deploy a sandbox, verify that the deployment is healthy, and rollback or alert on failure.

## 3. Data Flow: Lifecycle of a Release Event

1. **Discovery:** A Go worker polling Docker Hub detects a new base image tag for a configured source (e.g., the "Docker Hub" source under the "Go Runtime" project). Source-level exclusion filters (`exclude_version_regexp`, `exclude_prereleases`) are applied â€” filtered versions are discarded immediately.
2. **Ingestion & Transaction:** The worker stores the raw upstream payload and executes a single database transaction to insert the record into `releases` (linked to its `source_id`) and enqueue a `notify_release` River job.
3. **Notification (Source-Level):** A River worker picks up the `notify_release` job, resolves source-level subscriptions, and sends alerts to configured notification channels (Slack, Discord, email, webhooks).
4. **Agent Rule Check:** The same notification worker evaluates the project's `agent_rules` against the new release version. If criteria match (major bump, security patch, version pattern), it enqueues an `agent_run` River job.
5. **Agent Analysis (Project-Level):** A River worker picks up the `agent_run` job. The ADK-Go agent gathers recent releases, consults context sources, and produces a `SemanticRelease` with a structured report (subject, risk level, changelog summary, status checks, urgency, recommendation).
6. **Broadcast:** PostgreSQL triggers fire `NOTIFY` payloads on release insert and semantic release completion, pushing SSE events to connected dashboard clients.
7. **Project-Level Notification:** When a semantic release is completed, project-level subscribers are notified with the AI-generated report.

## 4. Directory Structure

```text
/changelogue
â”śâ”€â”€ cmd/
â”‚   â”śâ”€â”€ server/          # Main Go application entry point
â”‚   â””â”€â”€ agent/           # Agent CLI â€” run agent analysis for a project
â”śâ”€â”€ internal/
â”‚   â”śâ”€â”€ api/             # REST API handlers, middleware, SSE broadcaster
â”‚   â”śâ”€â”€ ingestion/       # Polling workers (IIngestionSource: Docker Hub, GitHub Atom)
â”‚   â”śâ”€â”€ agent/           # ADK-Go agent for semantic release analysis
â”‚   â”‚   â””â”€â”€ openai/      # OpenAI-compatible LLM provider adapter
â”‚   â”śâ”€â”€ routing/         # Notification routing worker and channel implementations (Sender)
â”‚   â”śâ”€â”€ queue/           # River queue job definitions (NotifyJobArgs, AgentJobArgs)
â”‚   â”śâ”€â”€ db/              # Connection pool and schema migrations
â”‚   â””â”€â”€ models/          # Shared domain structs (Release, Project, SemanticRelease, etc.)
â”śâ”€â”€ web/                 # Next.js frontend application
â”‚   â”śâ”€â”€ app/             # App Router pages (dashboard, projects, releases, channels, etc.)
â”‚   â”śâ”€â”€ components/      # React components organized by domain
â”‚   â””â”€â”€ lib/             # Client utilities and API wrappers
â”śâ”€â”€ scripts/             # Integration test harness
â”śâ”€â”€ docs/                # Design documents and plans
â””â”€â”€ go.mod
```

(polling)"] --> B["PostgreSQL +
River Queue"] B --> C["Agent
(ADK-Go)"] C --> D["Routing
(channels)"] A -.- A1["Docker Hub
GitHub"] B -.- B1["Transactional Outbox
LISTEN/NOTIFY â†’ SSE"] C -.- C1["Gemini LLM
research tools"] D -.- D1["Slack Â· Discord
Email Â· Webhooks"] ``` Release insert and job enqueue happen in a single SQL transaction â€” zero-loss guarantee. See [ARCH.md](ARCH.md), [API.md](API.md), and [DESIGN.md](DESIGN.md) for the full design. ## Quick start **Prerequisites:** Go 1.25+, Docker, Node.js 20+ ```bash # Start Postgres and the server (NO_AUTH mode) make dev # In another terminal â€” start the frontend make frontend-install make frontend-dev ``` The API runs on `localhost:8080`, the dashboard on `localhost:3000`. ## Environment variables | Variable | Default | Purpose | |----------|---------|---------| | `DATABASE_URL` | `postgres://localhost:5432/changelogue?sslmode=disable` | PostgreSQL connection | | `LISTEN_ADDR` | `:8080` | HTTP server bind address | | `NO_AUTH` | _(unset)_ | Set to `true` to disable API key auth (development) | | `LOG_LEVEL` | `info` | Log level: `debug`, `info`, `warn`, `error` | | `LLM_PROVIDER` | `gemini` | LLM provider: `gemini` or `openai` | | `LLM_MODEL` | `gemini-2.5-flash` / `gpt-5.2` | Model name (default depends on provider) | | `GOOGLE_API_KEY` | _(empty)_ | Gemini API key (required when `LLM_PROVIDER=gemini`) | | `OPENAI_API_KEY` | _(empty)_ | OpenAI API key (required when `LLM_PROVIDER=openai`) | | `OPENAI_BASE_URL` | `https://api.openai.com/v1` | OpenAI-compatible API base URL | ## Project structure ``` cmd/ server/ Entry point â€” wires all layers together agent/ Agent CLI â€” run agent analysis for a project internal/ agent/ ADK-Go agent orchestrator, tools, and worker openai/ OpenAI-compatible LLM provider api/ REST API, SSE, middleware, auth db/ Connection pool and migrations ingestion/ Polling sources (Docker Hub, GitHub Atom) models/ Shared domain types queue/ River job definitions and client routing/ Notification channels and delivery worker web/ Next.js dashboard (React + Tailwind + shadcn) scripts/ Integration test harness ``` ## Extending More providers (npm, PyPI, Helm, etc.) and channels (PagerDuty, etc.) are planned. Adding one is a single-interface implementation: **Add a registry provider** â€” implement `IIngestionSource` in `internal/ingestion/source.go`: ```go type IIngestionSource interface { FetchNewReleases(ctx context.Context) ([]IngestionResult, error) } ``` **Add a notification channel** â€” implement `Sender` in `internal/routing/sender.go`: ```go type Sender interface { Send(ctx context.Context, channel models.NotificationChannel, msg Message) error } ``` ## Useful commands ```bash make build # go build -o changelogue ./cmd/server make test # go test ./... make vet # go vet ./... make lint # alias for vet make integration-test # full integration test (spins up its own Postgres) make db-reset # drop and recreate the local database make frontend-build # build Next.js static export make agent-dev # run agent CLI for a specific project make clean # remove binary, stop containers, delete volumes ```
